mnemonics.title: R.E.D. Model
mnemonics.content: R.E.D. = Requirements → Elaboration → Design. This mnemonic represents the flow from gathering requirements to analyzing them in detail, then creating the design. Think 'Red' as in a stoplight - you must complete requirements (red light) before moving to design (green light).

mnemonics.title: I Have To Document Requirements Clearly
mnemonics.content: I: Identify stakeholders | H: Hold interviews | T: Translate to user stories | D: Document in SRS | R: Review and validate | C: Create use cases | C: Check for completeness

mnemonics.title: Crazy Frogs Dance In Swamps
mnemonics.content: Crazy: Capture requirements | Frogs: Formulate | Dance: Design model | In: Implement | Swamps: Software | (Helps remember Requirements → Analysis → Design → Implementation phases)

acronyms.usage: Use when documenting all functional and non-functional requirements in a structured format
Use when referring to the entire process from requirements to maintenance
Use when creating visual diagrams during the design phase
Use when validating requirements with end users before final design approval
Use when specifying what the system should do
Use when specifying quality attributes like performance, security, usability
Use when analyzing requirements from three perspectives during requirements gathering

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a detective gathering clues from witnesses (stakeholders) at a crime scene (project). Each interview is collecting evidence that forms the complete picture of what the system must do.
Picture a chemist separating a mixture into its components. Analysis breaks down complex requirements into manageable parts, identifying conflicts like oil and water that need resolution.
Think of an architect's blueprint. Just as a building needs foundation, walls, and systems planned before construction, software needs architecture, modules, and interfaces designed before coding.
Imagine a spider web connecting requirements (threads) to design components (cross-points). Each connection shows how design elements fulfill specific requirements.
Picture a stage play. Actors (users) interact with the stage (system) through specific scenes (use cases), showing all possible interactions without technical details.

analogies.analogy: Like a wedding planner interviewing the couple to understand exactly what they want: venue, menu, decorations, guest list, budget - before planning any details. Missing requirements means disaster on the big day.
Like the features of a car: it must accelerate, brake, turn, carry passengers. These are the 'must work' functions that define what the vehicle essentially does.
Like the quality attributes of the same car: it must get 30km/liter (efficiency), crash-test 5-star (safety), have AC (comfort). These make the car good, not just functional.
Like planning a city's infrastructure before construction. Roads, power lines, water systems, public transport - all must be designed to work together, with room for future expansion.
Like a nutritional label on food. Just as the label traces ingredients to nutritional facts, traceability matrix links each requirement to its corresponding design element and test case.
